title: Circular Motion
learningObjectives: Define angular velocity, period, and frequency for circular motion
Explain why objects in circular motion require a centripetal force
Calculate centripetal acceleration and centripetal force
Identify the force providing centripetal acceleration in real situations
introText: When an object moves in a circle at constant speed, its velocity is constantly changing direction — meaning it is accelerating. This acceleration always points toward the centre of the circle and is called centripetal acceleration.

steps.heading: Circular Motion Quantities
steps.body:
For an object moving in a circle of radius r with constant speed v:

• **Period (T)** — time for one complete revolution (seconds)
• **Frequency (f)** — revolutions per second (Hz); f = 1/T
• **Angular velocity (ω)** — angle swept per second (rad/s); ω = 2π/T = 2πf
• **Speed** — v = rω = 2πr/T

Angular velocity ω connects the 'circular' quantities to linear speed. At the same ω, a larger radius means a faster linear speed.
steps.deepDive: The radian is a dimensionless unit. One full revolution = 2π radians ≈ 6.28 rad. So if an object completes 3 revolutions per second, ω = 3 × 2π ≈ 18.85 rad/s.

steps.heading: Centripetal Acceleration and Force
steps.body:
Because velocity is always tangent to the circle and the direction keeps changing, there must be a centripetal (center-seeking) acceleration:

**a_c = v²/r = ω²r**

By Newton's Second Law, a net force must cause this acceleration:

**F_c = mv²/r = mω²r**

This force always points toward the centre of the circle.

**Important:** Centripetal force is not a new type of force — it is the net inward force **provided by** real forces such as:
• Tension in a string (ball on a string)
• Gravity (satellite orbit)
• Normal force (car on a banked road)
• Friction (car turning on a flat road)

steps.heading: Circular Motion Simulator
steps.instructionText: Adjust radius, speed, and mass. Observe how centripetal acceleration and force change. Note the velocity (tangent) and acceleration (inward) arrows.
steps.deepDive: In vertical circular motion (like a roller coaster loop), the centripetal force requirement must be met at every point. At the top of the loop, gravity + normal force both point inward, so the minimum speed (when N = 0) satisfies mg = mv²/r → v_min = √(gr).

steps.questionText: A 0.2 kg ball on a 0.5 m string moves in a horizontal circle at 4 m/s. What is the tension in the string?
steps.explanation: F_c = mv²/r = 0.2 × 16 / 0.5 = 6.4 N. The tension provides the centripetal force.

steps.questionText: A satellite orbits Earth at radius r with period T. Which expression gives its orbital speed?

questions.questionText: A car (mass 1200 kg) travels at 15 m/s around a flat circular bend of radius 45 m. What friction force is required?
questions.explanation: F_c = mv²/r = 1200 × 225 / 45 = 6000 N. Friction provides the centripetal force.

questions.questionText: If the radius of circular motion is doubled while the speed is kept the same, what happens to the centripetal acceleration?
questions.explanation: a_c = v²/r. Doubling r (with v constant) halves a_c.
